Learning JS From Zero (Day 25): What Exactly Is Callback Hell?
Day 25 of getting reincarnated into this JS world. This one's a quick look at what Callback Hell actually is.

Callback Hell — in Chinese we call it 回呼地獄 — is something you run into constantly in real development. The core problem behind it is pretty simple: we want our events to run in the order we ask for. And the moment you start chaining things to enforce that order, Callback Hell shows up. Here's an example from something I built a while back.
A chunk of Callback Hell from my RagnarokShopV3 project
Because we want the code to follow a specific flow, we end up with a ton of
checks just to confirm whether the data came back or whether something finished processing.

And the thing is, debugging Callback Hell is genuinely painful. The biggest reason? There are just so many brackets. One slip and you delete a closing bracket you didn't mean to, and now you're hunting through it line by line.
If you don't deal with it at all, it ends up looking like a Hadouken — that classic pyramid shape.

So why does Callback Hell happen in the first place? In JavaScript it's tied to the language's characteristics, which is why if you Google "Callback Hell" you'll find a lot of results talking about JavaScript specifically. But here's the thing to keep in mind: Callback Hell isn't unique to JavaScript. It can show up in basically any language — it's just a name everyone settled on.
